data.title: Akamai: Growth Isn't The Problem - Profitability Is
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Akamai Technologies Inc. (AKAM) has evolved into a security and cloud platform, positioning it to tap into growing markets. However, its growth appears partly acquisition-driven, with a limited track record of organic growth above long-term GDP levels.

The changing business profile has also led to a declining gap between the gross profit margin and fixed cost margin (gap). This, in turn, led to declining returns. Unless Akamai can scale its security and cloud businesses to restore this gap, its prospects remain constrained. Given these challenges and the lack of a margin of safety, the stock does not appear attractive at current prices.

Evolving business

Akamai is a global edge cloud platform that provides cybersecurity, cloud computing, and content delivery services. Its products and services help enterprises run fast, secure, and scalable internet applications.

Akamai today operates in three main areas:

  • Security (cybersecurity platform). This business protects websites, apps, APIs, and users from cyberattacks.
  • Cloud computing / compute (edge + cloud platform). This provides infrastructure to build and run applications.
  • Delivery (content & application delivery). In simple terms, this business accelerates and distributes internet content globally

A decade ago, Akamai was mainly a content delivery company. But around 2018, it shifted into security, and then around 2020, it diversified further into the cloud/compute business.

This shift is illustrated in the left part of Chart 1 - the delivery business was the biggest revenue contributor 5 years ago. However, in 2025, this position went to the security business.

I would consider Akamai a global business, as the US only accounted for about half of its revenue. Refer to Chart 1, right.

Given this picture, it is probably more appropriate when forecasting its performance to look at the post-2020 picture.

Secondly, from a total addressable market perspective, it is probably more appropriate to look at three overlapping markets – cybersecurity, cloud computing, and content delivery network.

Market research, as exemplified by the following, indicates that these are high single-digit or double-digit growth sectors. This augurs well for Akamai.

“The global cybersecurity market was valued at USD 239.04 billion in 2024…growing at a CAGR of 9.7 % from 2025 to 2030” Market Data Forecast.

“The global cloud computing market size was estimated at USD 943.65 billion in 2025…growing at a CAGR of 16.0% from 2026 to 2033” Grand View Research.

“The global content delivery network market size was estimated at USD 31.52 billion in 2025… growing at a CAGR of 19.7% from 2026 to 2033” Grand View Research.

Structural change

Given the changing business profile, you should not be surprised to see structural changes in its unit economics.

As illustrated in the left part of Chart 2, there is a decade of decline in the gross profit margin. At the same time, we see an increase in the fixed cost margin post 2020.

The impact of this is that the gap between the gross profit margin and the fixed cost margin has narrowed. This gap reflects the profitability of the business. The peak gap was in 2020 at 13.9 %. This had reduced to 4.4 % in 2025.

According to Akamai, the decline in gross margin is mainly due to pricing pressure in its legacy delivery business as well as a shift in its product mix towards lower-margin cloud/compute services.

It would appear that higher spending on cloud infrastructure, security capabilities, R&D, and go-to-market expansion led to a higher fixed cost base relative to revenue after 2020. This is probably aggravated by the slower revenue growth post 2020. For example, from 2016 to 2020, revenue grew at 8 % CAGR but declined to a 5% CAGR from 2021 to 2025.

My view is that the narrowing of the gap is largely structural as it reflects Akamai’s shift toward cloud and security businesses with different margin profiles. Unless there is a change in the business strategy, I would not expect the gap to go back to the 2020 level.

However, part of the narrowing could also reflect a transition phase where costs have grown ahead of revenue. As such, I would not rule out the possibility that the gap could improve over time with scale and operating leverage, even without a major strategic change.

Notes to Chart 2:

a) Fixed cost covers Selling, General and & Administration, and Depreciation & Amortization.

b) Variable cost is defined as Cost of Sales excluding Depreciation & Amortization.

c) Contribution is defined as Revenue – Variable Cost with Contribution margin = Contribution/Revenue

The structural change helps explain why, while revenue continues to increase over the past decade, we see a decline in profits post-2020. Refer to the left part of Chart 3.

Over the past decade, revenue grew at 6.7 % CAGR. The company did not provide an annual breakdown of the sources of the revenue growth. But you can get a sense of this by comparing the amount spent on acquisitions and CAPEX.

From 2016 to 2025, the company incurred $ 5.0 billion on acquisitions and intangibles compared to $ 3.8 billion on CAPEX. Given that the CAPEX for the year included the CAPEX on operations acquired in the previous year, the picture is that a bigger part of the revenue growth came from acquisitions. This is not a credible organic growth.

I have earlier mentioned the declining profits. This profit pattern is reflected in the return pattern as per the right part of Chart 3. The 2025 ROIC (5 %) and ROE (9 %) are lower than the peak returns in 2021.

You will note that the ROIC declined more significantly than the ROE due to the treatment of cash. In my ROIC computation, I excluded cash, while the company has built up a significant cash balance - about $ 1.2 billion in 2025 compared to $ 0.8 billion in 2016. This increases the capital base supporting operations and depresses ROIC more noticeably.

When I compared the 2021 to 2025 average returns, I found that they were still higher than the respective cost of funds.

  • The average ROIC was 7.9 % compared to the current WACC of 7.2 %.
  • The average ROE was 11.5 % compared to the current cost of equity of 8.6%

This meant that the company managed to create shareholder value over the past 5 years.

But as shown in the right part of Chart 3, the returns have been declining since 2021, and unless the company can arrest the decline, there will be concerns over this shareholder’s value creation in the future. The declining gross profitability as shown in the left part of Chart 3 makes this very challenging.

Peer analysis

Given its mixed business profile, I compared Akamai’s performance with the following peers. These are companies whose businesses overlap with Akamai 3 businesses.

i) Cloudflare is a leading connectivity cloud company.

ii) Digital Ocean Holdings simplifies cloud computing with its infrastructure and managed offerings.

iii) Fastly edge cloud platform helps brands deliver online experiences through edge compute, delivery, and security.

iv) F5 has solutions spanning application optimization, application security, and reliability.

v) Zscaler provides a cloud security platform.

As shown in Table 1, Akamai is a mid-size player in terms of the 2025 revenue and ranked No. 2 for the revenue growth rate from 2020 to 2025.

Notes to Table 1:

a) Except for F5 (FYE Sep) and Zscaler (FYE July), all the others had Dec as the financial year-end.

b) The values in the peer analysis were based on the financial years.

I compared Akamai's peer performance based on several metrics shown in Charts 4 to 6. Overall, Akamai went from a top-tier profitability player to a more middle-of-the-pack one over the review period.

While it had stable growth, it experienced weakening margins, returns, and earnings relative to peers. Akamai started the period with high returns on capital and leading EBIT margins. However, both metrics declined, indicating erosion in operating efficiency and profitability. Furthermore, cash flow and earnings were also volatile.

Notes to Chart 4:

a) Akamai ranked No. 3 in terms of 2020 revenue. While it had one of the higher 2020 to 2025 revenue growth rates, it still ranked No.3 for the 2025 revenue. This probably meant that it did to gain market share from a ranking perspective.

b) Akamai generally occupied the middle position in terms of the debt capital ratio over the review period.

Notes to Chart 5:

a) Akamai ranked No. 2 from 2020 to 2023 in terms of the return on capital. But it deteriorated slightly to rank No. 3 from 2024 to 2025. On an overall average basis over the review period, Akamai ranked No. 2. Note that it was among the 3 panel companies that had an average positive return on capital.

b) Akamai initially has the best EBIT margin and has held onto this position for 3 years. But declined to rank No. 3 by 2025.

Notes to Chart 6:

a) On an overall average basis, Akamai ranked No. 3 for the unlevered free cash flow margin. But its ranking changed annually, with the best performance in 2022 (No. 2 rank) and the worst in 2025 (No. 6 rank).

b) Akamai was among the 2 panel companies that experienced a decline in the annual EPS between 2020 and 2025.

Financial position

I would rate its financial position as moderately sound, as its positive points were partly offset by the negative ones. Its positive points included the following:

i) As of the end of Dec 2025, it had $ 1.2 billion in cash and short-term investments, equivalent to 10 % of its total assets.

ii) Over the past decade, it has generated positive cash flow from operations annually. Over this period, it generated $ 12.0 billion cash flow from operations compared to the total PAT of $ 4.6 billion. This is a good cash conversion rate.

iii) From 2016 to 2025, it generated enough cash flow from operations to support its capital expenditure. Excess was returned to shareholders. This is a good capital allocation plan. Refer to Table 2.

My concerns included the following:

i) It had a debt capital ratio of 53 % at the end of Dec 2025. This has increased from its 2016 low of 17%. But given its cash position and its cash generation ability, as illustrated in Table 2, I would not worry about this.

ii) From 2016 to 2025, it had an average annual reinvestment rate (reinvestment/NOPAT) of 114%. I defined Reinvestment = CAPEX & Acquisition – Depreciation & Amortization + increase in Net Working Capital.

This is not a sustainable rate, and over the long run, I would expect this to come down to one driven by the fundamental growth equation of growth = reinvestment rate X return.

To be fair, this high reinvestment rate is due to the acquisitions, as illustrated in Table 2. So, if the acquisition slows down, we would see a decline in the reinvestment rate.

Prospects

Akamai's prospects depend on addressing 2 issues:

a) Will the company be able to deliver topline organic growth greater than the long-term GDP growth rate of 4%, given its historical performance vis-à-vis market prospects?

b) Will Akamai be able to improve the gap between gross profit margin and fixed cost margin?

In its 2025 Annual Report, the company stated that 2025 revenue growth was driven by continued demand for its security and cloud computing solutions. It is also expanding its compute platform and positioning itself for AI-related workloads.

The challenge is that this growth has to offset the continuing decline in the delivery business. At the same time, part of the 2025 revenue growth was due to acquisitions.

Given these, I do not see enough evidence to assume that Akamai can sustain organic growth above 4%.

With regards to the second issue, the company reported that it is redeploying resources and pursuing its profitability objectives. It also took steps in 2025 to improve sales performance and better align incentives with growth areas.

The picture is that the company is trying to improve the gap, but the report also shows that the structural cost pressures are still very much in place.

The conclusion is that Akamai’s prospects are constrained by modest organic growth potential and structurally tighter margins.

Valuation

Notwithstanding Akamai challenging prospects, I have adopted a bit more optimistic outlook in valuing the company. The key assumptions used in the valuation are:

The company will reach a steady stage immediately with revenue growth a 4% in perpetuity. The base revenue would be the 2025 revenue.

Revenue growth would be organic with reinvestment governed by the fundamental growth equation.

Its EBIT margin would be based on the 2021 to 2025 average margin. In other words, I assumed that it would improve its 2025 margin by about 25%.

The capital turnover would be based on the 2021 to 2025 average values, suggesting a 13% improvement over the 2025 value.

The tax rate would be based on the past decade's average value.

On such a basis I obtained an intrinsic value of $ 51 per share compared to the market price of $ 117 per share (3 April 2026). There is no margin of safety.

Valuation model

I valued Akamai based on a single-stage Free Cash Flow to the Firm (FCFF) valuation model as shown in Table 3.

The WACC was based on the Google Search for “Akamai Technologies WACC” as summarized in Table 4.

Risks and limitations

I would consider my valuation an optimistic one, as the model would result in a return on capital of 8 % and an EBIT margin of 19 %. This is not only comparable to the past 5 years’ values but would place Akamai in the No. 2 position compared to the 2025 peer performance, as shown in Chart 4.

A reverse-engineered analysis showed that the current market price is based on a 27% EBIT margin, all else remaining unchanged. At this EBIT margin, this is 80% higher than the 2025 margin and higher than the past 5 years' peak 23% margin.

More importantly by assuming a growth that is fully organic, I have reduced the reinvestment significantly. For example, the reinvestment in the model is about 8% of revenue compared to the past decade 17%. This lower reinvestment meant a larger free cash flow to the firm thus leading to a higher valuation.

The valuation assumptions appear optimistic relative to my earlier analysis regarding margin expansion and capital efficiency (reinvestment) improvements. Yet given this optimistic picture, there is no margin of safety. You can understand why I think that the market has overpriced the company.

Note that the 2026 company guidance still points to only mid-single-digit topline growth with CAPEX at 23 % of revenue. In 2025, the company incurred about 21% of revenue for CAPEX and acquisitions (including intangibles). This is in line with my view that there is not enough evidence for stronger organic growth.

Secondly, the company guided to 26% to 28% non-GAAP operating margin in 2026, compared to the 2025 full-year non-GAAP operating margin of 30%. This suggests that structural cost pressure remains.

Conclusion

Over the past decade, Akamai has repositioned itself from a content delivery network provider into a broader platform spanning cybersecurity, cloud computing, and edge infrastructure.

While this transition exposed it to structurally growing markets, a significant part of this topline growth was due to acquisitions. At the same time, the shift has also brought changes in its unit economics. There was a narrowing gap between gross profit margin and fixed cost margin. This led to declining returns on capital and weaker profitability relative to peers.

Looking ahead, Akamai’s prospects depend on its ability to drive organic growth and narrow the gap. Despite an optimistic picture of organic growth and gap expansion, my valuation did not provide any margin of safety.

This is not an investment opportunity from a long-term value investment opportunity.

data.title: Willdan: A Drop That The Numbers Do Not Explain
data.description:
A predictable business

In front of us we have a company that executes the part that state regulators and utilities cannot do on their own. For example, the CPUC in California, the PSC in New York or the PUC in Nevada have, by law, certain energy efficiency targets for the utilities in their jurisdiction, that is, how many kWh they have to reduce, within what timeframe and under what budget they have to achieve it.

In addition, these budgets are a legal obligation that is financed through the tariffs that the utility's customers pay every month, so they have to be spent no matter what, and this is where Willdan Group (WLDN) model comes in, as these companies do not have the operational capacity to execute that operation, since programs must be designed, energy audits must be carried out across thousands of buildings, subcontractors managed, reports submitted to the regulator… so that "problem" is tendered out for someone else to solve it, that is, companies like WLDN.

In this way, this model generates visibility that, in my opinion, is unusual for a services company, since contracts with utilities have durations of between 3 and 5 years with funding already approved by the regulatory cycle. This means that if we take into account that at the end of FY25, the backlog was $1B on revenue of $681M, WLDN has coverage of 1.47 years, that is, even if it did not win another contract, it would have a secured pipeline for that period of time. A number that, above 1, I would already consider healthy.

But in addition, the regulatory budgets that feed this backlog have even longer horizons, such as the one approved by the CPUC of $4.3B from 2024 to 2027, which also includes another $4.6B until 2031, or the NY PSC with $5.36B until 2030. And this, as they are not federal allocations, makes it immune to the potential swings that could arise from Washington.

Although, as you may be sensing, this advantage can also simultaneously be a risk, and that is that the company cannot grow beyond what regulators budget, so reductions in programs or the non-renewal of a utility means that there is no alternative market to which to redirect that capacity.

That is why I want to determine whether the drop in WLDN since the end of January from $135 to $80 as of the time of writing the article corresponds to expectations in the regulatory cycle that would represent headwinds for the company.

Does the regulatory cycle explain the drop?

Reviewing the FY25 numbers presented by WLDN in recent days, they are not those of a company with a deteriorating regulatory cycle. Revenue has grown by 20.5% to $681M, and the gross margin also continues to grow, reaching 37.5%. If we combine this with adjusted EBITDA, which reaches 21.8% on net revenue, exceeding the 20% target set by management, it seems that the market is pricing in a future regulatory problem, as the numbers appear to be going very well.

So my next step has been to keep digging, and I have found that regarding the EPS guided for 2026, they project a range of $4.50 to $4.70, coming in below the adjusted EPS of this FY25 of $4.89 despite the fact that the guided EBITDA is around $85M-$90M, higher for this FY26. What does the company say about it?:

"the first big assumption is that consistent with the One Big Beautiful Bill, the 179D is set to expire at the end of June for this year. So that inability to carry that through to the end of the year means that we can only take advantage of that through projects that are started within the first 6 months of 2026"

To which they add the following:

"There's also a little bit of a shift in just the work that we're doing from the Clark County School District... to the shift into work we're doing for Alameda County and San Diego, which will involve fewer buildings, which are really the source of a lot of those 179D deductions that we got."

The translation is as follows, this deduction has contributed to a tax benefit of around $12M, thus turning the $40M of pre-tax income into $52.6M of net income. So for this 2026, with the deduction expiring in June, we will have an effect that is going to be materially reduced, or in other words, despite the operating business growing as shown by the guided EBITDA, net income will not.

But this is a one-off tax effect, not something similar to a deterioration of the business or the regulatory cycle. So the reason has to be somewhere else.

Perhaps a rotation from the Clark County School District is being priced in? This is a client that, together with SCE, accounted for around 23% of consolidated revenue in this FY25, and represents a set of school buildings. Translation, these buildings generate a high volume of 179D deductions as they are for public use, so when rotating towards municipal programs, there will be fewer buildings that qualify for those deductions, so the tax profile is affected.

Even so, the regulatory budgets of CPUC and NY PSC that feed the core remain active and intact, and the only thing that seems to be changing is the tax benefit that disappears in June, and a rotation that is being replaced by already signed larger contracts. Could it simply be noise then?

Competition seems to have the answer

My hypothesis is that this may be the key. ICF International (ICFI) is a competitor that generates around $1.9B annually, of which almost a third comes from energy efficiency programs for utilities, a vertical where WLDN generates nearly 85% of its business. So with 9,000 employees and a backlog of $3.4B in this FY25 growing at 24% YoY, we are not talking about a small startup.

In addition, the problem may come from where it is growing, since in Q4 of FY25, SCE granted ICF a contract modification to increase its role in agricultural energy efficiency programs, and although it is true that ICF was already involved with SCE, it now gains even more relevance.

For now they are different segments, but that is today, in the next bidding cycle both will compete for the same budgets allocated by the CPUC, so although there is no evidence that WLDN has lost the commercial contract with SCE, what is clear is that there is a competitor that is gaining ground.

But in my opinion, with the commercial program that WLDN manages with SCE, which to put into context delivered around $63.8M in system benefits in 2024, representing double the regulatory target of $28.4M, the performance delivered gives it enough strength to arrive strongly at the same table where there will be an ICF with more muscle than before.

Even so, I do not think it is enough to go from trading at $130 to the current $80, but it is a risk that is worth monitoring.

So what do we do with Willdan?

At $80 per share currently for WLDN, it trades at the same multiples as the closest peers I have identified, namely the aforementioned ICF, Tetra Tech (TTEK), and Ameresco (AMRC).

If we only take the multiple into account, it could be said that WLDN is fairly priced as it trades in line with the sector in general, but if we look at EBITDA growth, we have a business growing above its comparables, and yet the market assigns the same multiple to all of them.

By this I mean that, in my view, there is a mismatch between WLDN's growth and its current multiple, rather than pointing to an excessive undervaluation of the company, since if we consider the 13.6x EV/EBITDA FWD, we are not looking at a company trading at a distressed price. Now, I do consider it cheap on a relative basis, since with EBITDA growth of 40% over three consecutive years, a balance sheet with virtually no debt, and the current regulatory cycle, I do think it may be affected by a general "discount" that the market applies without distinguishing between companies with growth and those without.

Although this discount may be driven by fear around 179D, since even though the tax situation is already priced in, and a lower EPS than FY25 according to guidance, there is still the possibility that Congress does not renew 179D permanently beyond 2026. In that scenario, a structural deterioration for normalized EPS would need to be reflected.

This makes sense if we consider that the company's competitive advantage revolves around public building projects in schools and municipalities, for example, and it is these projects where 179D deductions make contracts more economically attractive. With 179D eliminated for projects starting from June 2026, this may be one of the reasons the market is pricing it in.

And as an extra, another variable that, although in my opinion is not being priced in currently, will start to gain more weight and will be interesting to see how companies deal with it: the labor force.

The electrical subcontractor market in the United States suffers from a structural deficit of new entrants versus retirements, as currently around 10,000 retire each year compared to 7,000 new entrants. Taking into account that 20% of the current workforce is over 55 years old, we have an estimated 81,000 job openings through 2034.

This is something I am not yet pricing in, but if you add competitive pressure, the removal of 179D deductions, and a labor market under pressure year after year, it may result in a combination that temporarily compresses the multiple.

My Takeaway

For me, taking into account the regulatory cycle and the numbers they have reported in this FY25, they do not fully justify such a sharp drop in price given the current state of the business, and it may be interesting to start building a position from here.

It is true that the 179D tax effect has an influence, but it is something one-off, I do not think this is going to mean a structural deterioration for the company. In addition, the rotation from Clark County is not a loss, so it should not be considered a negative factor either.

Perhaps competitive pressure could be a risk to monitor with ICF gaining strength in agricultural energy efficiency, but WLDN is also executing very competitively, so I believe it is something to watch but not a risk that should already be priced into the stock.

From a price perspective, I can observe how it is stabilizing at a very relevant trading volume over the past year, and it has held the $70 level very well, so at these levels it may be very interesting to start building a position, with downside protection if the $70 level is lost with strength, as there would be nothing below to support it until $58-$60 per share.

data.title: Meta Platforms: Time To Double Down
data.description:
Last time I covered Meta Platforms, Inc. (META) back in August, when I published my most recent bullish article on the company. In that article, I argued that the company’s massive bets on AI were likely going to transform the business for the better. While the company’s stock has since that time depreciated by ~25%, the business itself has never been in a stronger position than it is today.

The recent earnings report exceeded expectations, the AI investments are beginning to yield decent returns, and the guidance for Q1 suggests that Meta still has plenty of room for growth. While the stock took a beating in the last couple of months, I attribute that more to the worsening of the overall macro environment and not to the company-specific developments. Because of that, I remain optimistic about Meta’s future despite the recent underperformance of its shares.

There's Nothing Not To Like About Meta

The latest earnings report that was released earlier this year showed that Meta’s business is thriving. In Q4, the revenues increased by 23.8% Y/Y to $59.89 billion and exceeded the expectations by $1.42 billion. Thanks to such results, Meta was able to generate over $200 billion in annual revenues for the first time in its history. While the operating margin decreased from 48% to 41% in Q4 due to the rising costs primarily caused by the increase in spending on AI, I’m not viewing this as a major red flag.

After all, all that spending on AI and its advertising engine is already starting to yield some decent results for Meta. During the latest earnings call, Meta’s management noted that the ad impressions were up 18%, while the average price per ad was up 6%, thanks to the better performance of ads. The ad clicks and conversions have also improved as Meta doubled the GPU training capacity for its GEM recommendation model last quarter. More importantly, the company has also redistributed ads across users and sessions on Facebook in the second half of 2025. Thanks to this, the revenue impact was almost four times larger than simply increasing the ad load.

As for Meta’s other projects that have been launched within its core products, the video generation tools for advertisers hit a $10 billion run rate in Q4. This translates to a growth of almost three times faster than the overall ad revenue. In addition, the incremental attribution feature has now reached a multi-billion-dollar run rate after driving a 24% increase in conversions. It was able to do so just in seven months since its launch.

Therefore, while Meta’s spending has been on the rise recently and has had an impact on its margins, the company’s top-line performance has nevertheless exceeded expectations, while the performance of its ad products has also improved.

What’s also important to mention is that, in addition to Facebook and Instagram, the company’s other platforms are also starting to generate sizable revenues for Meta. In my previous articles on Meta, I was saying how the monetization of WhatsApp and Threads has the potential to improve the company’s performance and create additional income sources over time. That’s exactly what has started to happen recently. Earlier this year, Meta finally started running ads on Threads. Using Meta’s AI-powered Advantage+ program, advertisers now have the option to access over 400 million monthly active users that the platform has.

At the same time, at the end of last quarter, WhatsApp paid messaging crossed a $2 billion annual run rate. Last year, Barclays estimated that Threads and WhatsApp could add up to $6 billion in incremental ad revenue this year and $19 billion in 2027. Such a potential boost to revenues should help Meta once again to scale its business and exceed expectations in the years to come.

More importantly, there’s a possibility that the bottom-line performance is going to improve even more in the foreseeable future. My biggest issue with Meta for a couple of years was its relentless spending on its metaverse projects. This has only resulted in losses and had a negative impact on the company’s overall profits. Even the latest earnings report showed that the company’s Reality Labs division, which is responsible for all the metaverse projects, generated $2.21 billion in revenue and made $19.19 billion in operating losses last year.

The good news is that a few months ago, it was reported that Meta had cut around 10% of its Reality Labs staff, which is a sign that the company is scaling down its metaverse ambitions. With core business improving and the needless spending potentially being scaled down, there’s a chance for Meta to once again exceed its revenue and earnings expectations in the following quarters. Considering that its Q1 revenue guidance of between $53.5 billion and $56.5 billion was already above the street’s estimates of $51.41 billion, it makes sense to assume that Meta has plenty of growth opportunities, and there’s nothing not to like about the company at this stage.

What's Next For Meta's Shares?

Back in August, my discounted cash flow model showed that Meta’s fair value is $790.43 per share. Since the company has released several earnings reports since that time and the street has revised some of its assumptions, it makes sense to update that model. Back then, I assumed that Meta’s revenue growth rate in FY25 and beyond would be 20% Y/Y. In reality, Meta’s FY25 revenues exceeded my expectations and were up 22.2% Y/Y, while the consensus on the street is that in FY26 the growth rate will be nearly 25%. As such, I have also increased my FY26 revenue growth rate assumptions to 25%, after which the growth rate is expected to be 20% in all the following years.

As for the EBIT margin, it turned out to be slightly lower than I expected due to rising costs, which were discussed earlier in this article. Despite this, there’s a potential for the margin to gradually improve over time as the potential workforce cut, along with the scaling down of metaverse projects, has the potential to improve the bottom-line performance.

The tax rate in the model is 14.5%, which is the midpoint of the management’s latest tax rate expectations in the range of 13% and 16%. The CapEx assumptions have been increased and, for FY26, are within the management’s expected range. As a result of the CapEx increase, the D&A assumptions have also been increased. The assumptions for the change in net working capital are not far from the 2025 results.

Under the new assumptions, my discounted cash flow (DCF) model shows that Meta’s fair value is $800.59 per share. This suggests that Meta’s stock remains greatly undervalued after the latest depreciation.

On top of that, the Street is even more optimistic about Meta’s future, considering that the current average price target is $860.25 per share.

The Risks Haven't Disappeared

Despite being undervalued, Meta's stock has been under pressure for a while now. Some of that is a result of the broader rotation of investors from tech names to safer stocks. This change in sentiment started last November, and I covered it briefly in one of my articles about another tech company. Considering that Meta, like a number of other major tech firms, has significantly increased its CapEx to expand its AI capabilities, investors started to decrease their exposure to growth companies due to the rising overspending risks.

Earlier in this article, I mentioned the decrease in the operating margin that Meta has experienced due to rising costs. There are now even reports that the company could cut up to 20% of its workforce due to rising AI expenses. While so far, Meta’s investments in AI have helped the company’s advertising business to thrive, there’s no guarantee that it will remain the case in the future. That’s why in the current turbulent macro environment, stocks of Meta and its peers have been underperforming for a while. What’s worse is that there’s a risk that they will continue to perform poorly despite the solid growth of the underlying businesses if geopolitical tensions continue to rise.

The Bottom Line

Despite the recent underperformance of Meta’s stock, the company’s business is in the best shape it has ever been. Revenues are on the rise, the growth is accelerating, and the rising spending on AI so far has been beneficial for Meta. On top of that, the monetization of Threads and WhatsApp, along with the scaling down of the company’s metaverse ambitions, should help the company improve its top-line and bottom-line performance at the same time. Considering this, I continue to view Meta as a solid long-term investment, especially at the current price.

data.title: Reinsurance Group of America: Baby Bonds Offer Solid Yield
data.description:
We have personally traded Reinsurance Group of America, Incorporated (RGA), multiple times during the last five years. At times, it has even been our largest holding. All of our trades have been on the long side, and our confidence in this business has been rewarded with rich dividends (figuratively and literally). As its name indicates, RGA is in the business of reinsurance, with a focus on life and health reinsurance products. Why do insurers need insurance?

RGA is one of the largest of its kind globally, with a presence in over 100 countries spanning 6 continents. As of the end of last year, it had around $4.3 trillion of life reinsurance in force and close to $157 billion in assets.

We first covered this company on this platform back in 2021, when it was our largest position.

Over time, we moved from the common stock to the baby bonds issued by this reinsurer. We spoke about Reinsurance Group of America, Incorporated SB DB FX/FL56 (RZB) in our June 2025 piece, where we detailed our rationale for taking a sizeable position in it. We have since sold out of it profitably. Today we will talk about one of its siblings.

Reinsurance Group of America, Incorporated NT CAL 52 (RZC)

This baby bond will be a familiar name to those that read our piece back in November 2022. Issued in 2020, RZC's coupon is 7.125%. It matures on October 15, 2052, but can be redeemed at any time after October 15, 2027, which is also its rate reset date.

The debentures will bear interest (i) from and including the date of original issue to, but excluding, October 15, 2027 (the “First Reset Date”) at the fixed rate of 7.125% per annum and (ii) from, and including, the First Reset Date, during each Reset Period, at a rate per annum equal to the Five-Year Treasury Rate as of the most recent Reset Interest Determination Date plus 3.456% to be reset on each Reset Date.

Source: RZC Prospectus

If the baby bond is not redeemed in October 2027, the reset rate, as calculated today, would be around 7.43% (3.97% risk-free rate plus 3.456% spread).

Certain conditions have to be met for the redemption to happen prior to the reset date, and those can be found in the prospectus. While highly improbable, investors should read up on those.

RZC pays out interest quarterly on the 15th of January, April, July, and October. Issued at $25, this baby bond is currently trading a little over par value at $25.13.

With the "ex-dividend" date just behind us (Apr 1), there is little to none of accrued interest built into the current price. We can use it directly to calculate the yield to call (ergo 1.5 years to maturity), which is 6.76%.

The specific credit ratings for RZC at the time of issuance can be seen here. RGA's recently issued subordinated notes (CUSIP: 759351AV1), which rank pari passu with RZC, received a rating of BBB+ from S&P, which would also apply to RZC.

Verdict

So here we have an investment-grade-rated baby bond issued by an A+ rated company. The actual bonds are subordinate but still garner a BBB+ rating and are yielding over 7% currently, with a yield to call of 6.76%. We always find such resetting bonds to be best compared to the yield of iShares 5-10 Year Investment Grade Corporate Bond ETF (IGIB) and iShares 10+ Year Investment Grade Corporate Bond ETF (IGLB), both of which, on average, are comprised of BBB-rated securities and yield around 5% and 5.5%, respectively. Yes, these bonds can be around for much longer than IGIB's ETF duration. But because they reset regularly, your duration risk is really small. We see the current spread here versus IGIB as extremely favorable. The last time we did a deep dive on this baby bond, we put it on our watch list and gave it a HOLD rating.

RZC is a good issue to keep your eyes on. We don't think the bond is particularly expensive considering its fundamentals, but we think there are better opportunities out there and currently this just makes it to our watch list.

Source: Reinsurance Group: A Look At The Newly-Issued Baby Bonds

It has now moved up from our watch list to become one of our buys.

Please note that this is not financial advice. It may seem like it and sound like it, but surprisingly, it is not. Investors are expected to do their own due diligence and consult with a professional who knows their objectives and constraints.

data.title: ConocoPhillips: Regional Constraints May Impact Long-Term Plan, Growth Remains Ahead
data.description:
ConocoPhillips (COP) is in a position of strength as oil and international gas prices remain elevated as a result of the supply chain disruption in the Strait of Hormuz. With pricing remaining strong and an appealing strip price throughout the remainder of eFY26, ConocoPhillips should realize an exceptionally strong fiscal year, particularly as the organization focuses on cost management while optimizing its asset base. Resulting from the war, ConocoPhillips may face a setback in Qatar as a result of regional gas facilities and fields being the targets of missile strikes, which may result in the reshaping of management’s free cash flow expectations for eFY27-28. Despite this factor, I believe ConocoPhillips is well positioned to realize strength over the coming years, particularly as it develops its Alaskan assets and leans into LNG export capacity. I am recommending COP shares with a Buy rating with a price target of $160/share at 5.25x eFY28 EV/aEBITDA.

ConocoPhillips Operational Update

ConocoPhillips is positioning itself to reduce its capital spending and operating expenses by $1b while expanding production in eFY26, reducing its free cash flow breakeven to the low $30/bbl WTI range by the end of the decade. The lower free cash flow breakeven is underpinned by lower preproduction capital deployment, particularly as it relates to the Willow project, which should shave off $6b in total capital spend. Through improved efficiencies, management is expecting to generate $1b in incremental free cash flow each year from eFY26-28 and an additional $4b resulting from Willow coming online in eFY29.

Willow is ConocoPhillips’ Alaska North Slope project in the National Petroleum Reserve on the western side of the region. The project has an estimated 600MMbbl of recoverable reserves and is expected to produce 180Mbbl/d at its peak. The project is expected to cost $7-7.5b, with first oil coming online in eFY29. As of Q4 ’25, the project is 50% complete with 4 wells fully permitted.

Despite the strong outlay for eFY26, ConocoPhillips may face some headwinds in the Middle East with the emerging Iranian war. Accordingly, natural gas assets in Qatar have been the target of missile strikes, impacting both infrastructure and gas fields in the region. As a result, Qatar has halted LNG output and declared force majeure on LNG shipments, taking the appropriate safety precautions to protect personnel in the region. The Iranian war may create substantial setbacks for ConocoPhillips’ North Field East (NFE) and North Field South (NFS) projects. As of Q4 ’25, NFE was 80% complete and was expected to start up in e2H26. If this project was severely impacted by the war in Iran, ConocoPhillips’ eFY27-28 $1b incremental free cash flow outlay could be significantly impacted, which I suspect we’ll gain more insight on in the Q1’26 earnings release on April 30, 2026.

As for U.S. operations, ConocoPhillips has commercial LNG offtake agreements for 10.2MTPA that are expected to commence between 2026-2031, with regasification capacity in Europe of 6.7MTPA. This, in part, will benefit from the ramp-up of the Port Arthur Liquefaction facility through its partnership with Sempra, where ConocoPhillips owns 30% of the equity interest.

As part of its asset optimization efforts, ConocoPhillips is considering the sale of $2b of Permian assets in the Delaware Basin acquired through deals with Concho Resources and Shell. This would follow the $2.5b in asset dispositions in 2025, nearing management’s target of disposing of $5b in assets by the end of eFY26.

ConocoPhillips Financial Position & Forecast

eFY26 will be another year of operational improvements and cost controls with the intent of driving down operational and capital costs with the target of achieving more than $1b in a cost-reduction run-rate. ConocoPhillips is expected to improve its operational structure in eFY26, reducing operating expenses by $400mm, in part resulting from operational synergies with its 2024 Marathon Oil acquisition.

For Q1 ’26, I’m forecasting ConocoPhillips to generate $16.6b in net revenue with a diluted EPS of $1.95/share. My basis for these estimates includes the strengthened oil prices towards the end of the quarter paired with improved cost management. Looking through eFY26, I’m forecasting oil prices to remain elevated in Q2 ’26 before normalizing to the low-to-mid-$70/bbl in e2h26 in accordance with the strip price. Higher oil prices should create substantial margin expansion for ConocoPhillips for e1H26 before normalizing while leaning more heavily into management’s cost-disciplined approach for operations in the second half of the fiscal year.

ConocoPhillips closed FY25 with $6.9b in cash & equivalents and $23.4b in debt on the balance sheet for a net leverage ratio of 0.65x net debt/aEBITDA. In FY25, ConocoPhillips paid down $900mm in debt, reducing its net debt position by $1.75b to $16.5b when compared to the close of FY24.

Risks Related to ConocoPhillips

Bull Case

ConocoPhillips is positioning its assets to focus more heavily on gas export, an appealing market as the war in Iran disrupts the international LNG cargo supply chain. While ConocoPhillips’ Qatar assets may be negatively impacted by the Iranian war, I believe the firm has capacity outside of the region that can partially offset these losses, especially as commodity prices remain elevated. Additional cost synergies post-Marathon acquisition as well as cost discipline should drive margins for the coming years.

Bear Case

ConocoPhillips’ Qatar assets may be negatively impacted by the war in Iran, potentially leading to a substantial impact on the planned cash generation for eFY27-28.

Valuation & Shareholder Value

ConocoPhillips returned 45% of operating cash flow to shareholders in FY25 in the form of share repurchases and dividend distributions, and is expecting to maintain this target for eFY26. This translates to $5b in shares repurchased in FY25 on top of the $4b returned through its dividend distribution. As of the close of FY25, ConocoPhillips had $25.7b remaining under its existing share repurchase authorization.

Overall, I believe ConocoPhillips is well positioned in the market as the organization reshapes its assets to focus on high-yielding production, particularly as it develops its Alaska assets. With the potential for increasing demand for U.S.-borne LNG cargoes, I believe ConocoPhillips will realize an appealing uplift in earnings potential over the coming years. Fundamentally, I believe COP shares should be priced at $160/share at 5.25x eFY28 EV/aEBITDA; I am recommending COP shares with a Buy rating.

Using the model: the valuation table above references my financial forecast in the firm’s “financial position” section and ties it to the stock’s historical trading premiums. The trading premium array is derived through the normal operating cycle, with the blue-sky scenario being the stock’s peak multiple and the gray-sky scenario being the lowest point. The target multiple aims for the midpoint, or the most likely trading range for the company’s stock. The trading multiples from there are set to a probability factor based on the likelihood of the stock trading at that premium based on its historical presence. From there, the trading multiple is tied to the probability factor to derive its relative market capitalization and relative multiple.

data.title: This Is A Most Compelling Buying Opportunity
data.description:
Do you remember when I said to go all in with an article during the darkest days of COVID? It turned out to be within days of the market bottom at that time. The market then went on a major bull market run shortly thereafter. That was accurate, market call #1.

How about back in January of 2023, when I said in another article that the Nasdaq had bottomed? It appeared to me at that time that the Fed was getting to pivot away from their draconian rate hikes, and I could see many major tech stocks and the Nasdaq itself putting in a very firm bottom. Lo and behold, it had! The Nasdaq went on a major bull run from that point. That was accurate, market call #2.

Last year, during the darkest days of the Tariff War that President Trump had declared against the world, I stepped in with another bold article right at the bottom of the market and once again said to go all in. With the S&P 500 at around 4,800 at the time, that turned out to be yet another terrific buying opportunity. That was accurate, market call #3.

I could rest on my laurels with three home runs, but now it is time for a grand slam. And now I’m coming up to bat again, and the field looks to be well in our favor. I’m seeing the Nasdaq down almost 14% from its Jan. 28 high and the S&P 500 down almost 10.0% during that same time period.

Fear and anxiety are once again running wild. So I’m swinging for the fences! I believe we are once again reaching another major bottom in the market! Which means it’s not time to sell scared; it’s time to buy!

Now, before you start booing at me from the rafters, let me explain. This isn’t a call I made based on emotions or headlines or even what the crowd is saying. I’ve come to this conclusion the same way I come to all of my conclusions: earnings, valuations, and forward reward potential.

And right now, while the market has just been falling to pieces, it has become clear to me that earnings have remained far stronger than most people seem to understand.

Now, the current mood in the market is dark, and that’s something anyone can see. We just got through some of the absolute nastiest weeks I have seen in a really long time. Friday, March 27th, was a bloodbath! The sentiment and technicals of the market looked very bad on that ugly, ugly day. The major indexes broke below their 200-day moving averages!

Now, you can absolutely make the argument that when the charts look as bad as they do right now, the market is either headed for a full bear market or… it is creating a very compelling buying opportunity. And I believe it's the latter.

You see, during my 26 years as a professional analyst and money manager, I’ve learned to cut through the noise and din of the market. Instead, I focus on the three most important criteria in the market overall: Earnings, Earnings, Earnings.

For Q1 2026 (which just ended), the estimated year-over-year earnings growth rate for the S&P 500 is now 13.2%. Did you know that, if this number holds, it will mark the sixth straight quarter of double-digit earnings growth?

Not only that, but Revenue growth for the first quarter is estimated at 9.7%! And net profit margins have remained strong as well, running at 13.2%! While the market is behaving like it's the end of the world around the corner, corporate earnings aren’t behaving that way at all!

You know what? Let's go even further!

For all of 2026, the earnings growth estimate is sitting around 13%. And for 2027, it’s around 17%. Both of these will be all-time, record earnings hauls!

The market isn’t pricing in that growth at all. In fact, it’s doing the opposite! Prices have consistently been falling while earnings estimates have been climbing upwards! Just look at the graph below.

Don’t you see how powerful of a combination that is? It’s exactly the kind of thing that I look for when I’m trying to identify a major low, and it's also why valuation matters so much now.

The forward P/E on the S&P 500 has dropped to 19.8, and not that long ago it was around 23.0. The five-year average is 19.9, so after this washout, we will be trading below the five-year average forward P/E multiple. That means the market has become much cheaper, and the earnings base has remained resilient! That’s a setup I associate with an important bottom forming.

And if you want the upside to look even more compelling, all you have to do is run the numbers.

The market is currently using the S&P 500’s 2027 earnings of $372.45 as its guidepost. If we just apply a simple multiple of 20X, we get a 12-month target price of around 7,400.

If we get back to the 22-23X that we were trading at earlier this year, we get to 8,000 and beyond. Right now, the consensus 12-month target price on the street (of which I am a part) is just over 8,300.

This represents some serious upside potential.

I mean, come on, how long has it been since we’ve seen that kind of reward potential? You’d have to go back to this same time last year to find a setup this compelling!

This is the heart of my argument! The numbers.

Now, of course, we have to be aware of the risks. Oil prices are elevated right now ($110 bbl), and the 10-year Treasury has been stubbornly high (4.31%). These two factors are a big reason for the compression in the market multiple (19.8X). Keep in mind, however, that the earnings picture has only gotten better as the market has been selling off.

Geopolitical headlines aren’t exactly looking pretty either, and sentiment is certainly weak. But what if this proves to be just another external shock? And not a structural collapse in earnings. That would mean exactly what I’m saying right now, that the market is giving investors one of the best buying opportunity windows in a year!

Now, to be fair, the current tariff war might have some impact on earnings, especially in the short term. But I truly believe that there is a long-term foundation being built here, and it's one that’s stronger than the market currently appreciates.

I mean, we are talking about predictable taxes, reasonable regulations, lower interest rates, more responsible government spending, and a stronger manufacturing base. Those are all things that I can see supporting both the economy and the stock market over time.

And let me be very clear. I’m not saying the next move happens in a straight line or anything. I’m also not saying this has to be the exact day of the low or the bottom.

What I am saying is that the conditions for a major buying opportunity are being formed at this moment. And yes, I know, the charts look terrible. But they looked terrible in early 2023 during COVID, and they looked terrible last year during the tariff tantrum.

What I’m saying is that when technicals get this washed out, all while valuations improve and earnings remain intact, the market is far closer to a bottom than the crowd realizes.

And if you still don't see it, go back and look at those numbers again.

I think too many people are scared and focusing on the daily news from this current crisis instead of recognizing how quickly things can change. Markets turn before the news improves!

They don’t call for a timeout at the bottom to let you know what’s going on. And they certainly don’t wait for everyone to feel comfortable again. They only move once valuations improve and earnings prove more durable than expected. And isn’t that the exact kind of backdrop we have right now?

When I sit back and take a look at the whole, entire picture and put everything together. I come to a very straightforward conclusion.

This is not the time to panic.

The market may be acting as if the bad news will last forever. It won’t.

The market may be acting as if this current market shock will never calm down. It will.

As always, be sure and choose your individual buys wisely, however.

data.title: Atlas Lithium Stock Rebounded After Cooperation Agreement: I Rate It A Buy
data.description:
I last wrote about Atlas Lithium Company (ATLX) on Seeking Alpha on January 7, 2025. I explained in that article why this lithium producer could be an attractive takeover candidate. ATLX shares traded at $7.54 on January 7, 2025, and fell below $4 before the recent recovery. While ATLX remains below the early 2025 price level, I continue to believe the company offers value to a variety of potential suitors.

The Japan-U.S. Critical Cooperation Agreement Includes ATLX’s Flagship Project in Brazil

On April 2, 2026, Atlas Lithium’s 100%-owned Neves project in Brazil’s Lithium Valley was named in the Joint Fact Sheet for Japan-U.S. Critical Minerals Project Cooperation and is the only Brazil-based lithium project named in the list. The fact sheet spells out that inclusion could be related to Mitsui & Company’s equity interest in the project. The U.S. and Japanese governments are considering financial support for the development of the lithium project in Minas Gerais, Brazil.

The daily chart of ALTX shares shows they jumped after the April 2 announcement and formed a bullish key reversal. ATLX shares traded in a $4.35 to $4.58 range on April 1 and a $4.28 to $4.70 range on April 2, closing at $4.65, above the April 1 high. A bullish reversal occurs when a stock or asset trades below the prior period’s low and closes above the previous high.

Marc Fogassa, CEO and Chairman of Atlas Lithium, said:

The inclusion of the Neves Project in the Japan-U.S. Critical Minerals Joint Fact Sheet, with both the U.S. and Japanese governments considering financial support for our project, is a powerful recognition of the strategic value of our assets and the progress that our team has achieved. Together with our strategic partner Mitsui & Co., we remain focused on bringing the Neves Project into production and supplying high-quality lithium concentrate to the global market. We are also proud of working with the local communities in bringing progress to an economically disadvantaged area of Brazil.

ATLX Shares Reached a Bottom in Mid-December and Made a Higher Low on March 23

The weekly chart of ATLX shares highlights a pattern of higher lows.

After reaching a low of $3.54 per share on April 7, 2025, following the Trump administration’s “Liberation Day” tariff announcement, the shares rose to $8.25 in mid-October 2025. The volatile stock then corrected to a higher low of $3.70 in mid-December 2025 before rising to $6.72 in late January 2026. The latest decline took ATLX shares to $3.96 during the week of March 23, 2026, before the latest rally to a closing price of $4.65 per share on April 2.

The pattern of higher lows and inclusion in the Japan-U.S. Critical Minerals Project could support a rally in ATLX that challenges the mid-October 2025 high of $8.25 per share.

Lithium Prices Are Climbing - ATLX Beat Estimates in the Latest Earnings Report

A March 23, 2026, article on Carboncredits.com highlighted that

“The lithium market is experiencing a major rebound due to rising demand and tightening supply. Battery-grade lithium carbonate spot prices have jumped to about $24,086 per metric ton, based on data from Shanghai Metals Market. This markets a sharp increase from earlier lows in 2025, after a period of oversupply had weighed on the market.”

The chart highlights that lithium carbonate prices (LC:COM) have more than doubled since mid-2025, reaching their most recent 2026 high. Lithium demand has grown as a growing number of utilities, data centers, and industrial users adopt battery storage applications, expanding lithium’s addressable market beyond electric vehicles.

The chart shows that the lithium market has shifted from oversupply in 2024 and 2025 to a slight deficit in 2026, which is forecast to grow over the coming years. Atlas Lithium has targeted the 2026-2027 window for its first production of lithium concentrate at the Neves Project.

The chart shows that when ATLX announced its latest quarterly earnings on March 4, 2026, it beat analyst estimates for EPS and revenues, a positive development for the junior lithium miner.

Seeking Alpha Factor Grades- Better on valuation, worse on profitability

At the time of my early 2025 Seeking Alpha article on ATLX, the company’s Seeking Alpha Factor Grades were as follows:

The most recent Factor Grades are as follows:

Growth remains at the highest possible A+ grade, while valuation has remained at a C. As ATLX continues to report negative EPS, profitability declined from an A to a D-, and the stock’s performance has been slightly upgraded from a failing grade to a C- in the momentum category.

With lithium output on the horizon this year and next, ATLX could be priced attractively at below $5 per share.

An Even More Attractive Takeover Candidate in April 2026

My early 2025 evaluation of ATLX mentioned two factors that justify my former and current buy recommendations. I suggested that commodity cyclicality favored lithium in 2025, and the commodity’s price has indeed risen. Moreover, I felt that ATLX’s market cap made it a takeover candidate. At $4.51 per share, ATLX has a current market cap of $126.18 million. Meanwhile, the top lithium producers with significant market caps could have ATLX on their M&A radar. In early 2025, the top market caps were as follows:

On April 6, these companies had the following market caps:

Albemarle: $20.99 billion

Sociedad Química y Minera de Chile: $23.77 billion

Ganfeng Lithium: $23.04 billion

Tianqi Lithium: $13.74 billion

Mineral Resources: $7.13 billion

Pilbara Minerals: $11.29 billion

Arcadium Lithium: $6.28 billion

Liontown Resources: $3.71 billion.

All of these lithium producers have experienced significant increases in market capitalization since early 2025. At a market cap of $126.18 million, ATLX remains a juicy takeover target that could deliver accretive results for any of the leading multi-billion-dollar-cap lithium producers. I continue to rate ATLX a Buy at under $5 per share.

data.title: Blackstone: Valuation Compression Masks Intact Earnings Power
data.description:
Blackstone Inc. (BX) looked like a Buy to me last May. Alternatives were structurally gaining share and continue to be a relevant theme today as macro uncertainties have risen considerably (from the tariff uncertainties in May 2025) and equity markets have cooled. I expected Blackstone to fully strengthen its Fee Related Earnings (FRE), leveraging its unmatched scale and distribution.

And when we look at operating data since then, almost every box does look ticked. FRE has grown, AUM has scaled, and Distributable Earnings (DE) have also increased strongly. Despite the strength, the stock is down ~15% from the time of my Buy thesis (total returns including reinvested dividends). It did initially rally by 35-40%, but the retracement from the top is even sharper than the net-net correction from the time of my May thesis.

The corrections are definitely a sharp valuation compression rather than a drop in fundamentals, which means markets are viewing earnings differently today. And the bulk of the compression has happened in 2026, keeping with wider market cooling, risk-off sentiments, and increasing macro fears, especially stagflation. That pressures the realized earnings timing visibility for Blackstone.

This analysis walks through the valuation compression and reasons and concludes that while the performance-linked earnings (or carry) could be temporarily weak (as exit uncertainties increase in a pressured macro scenario), they will normalize over time. Which means the valuation compression is an opportunity to accumulate. Because the gap between earnings reality and valuation has widened in comparison to May last year, Blackstone becomes a higher-conviction Buy in my view.

Core Engine Is Compounding

FY2025 FRE was reported as ~$5.7b, up 9% YoY, driven by ~11% growth in fee-paying AUM and ~$239b of inflows. The quality of earnings also looks good, with a growing share of the capital tagged long duration or perpetual. Perpetual capital is now 48% of the fee-earning AUM, and that is up by 18% YoY.

There is a broader mix shift in the works, with credit and insurance gaining share. Credit and insurance, along with private equity, registered the strongest gains through the year (both ~18% up YoY), but credit and insurance had a larger base, implying a higher share of the AUM increase. This should mean more stable and recurring fees, although immediate carry could be lower. That should balance out near-term volatility concerns by introducing more certainty for the base earnings. The trade-off could be that carry could be slightly deferred, although not diminished.

DE was also up in 2025, with DE up 19% (~$7.1b), indicating cash conversion mechanics remain strong. DE growing faster than FRE indicates a strong monetization environment - in fact net realizations were up ~50% for the full year.

Q4 FRE did see some drop, but that seems to be a function of a mechanical distortion rather than any signal of underlying weakness. Excluding the impact of $1b recognized in Q4 2024 from infrastructure crystallizations, FRE would have actually shown strong YoY growth (~24%).

The Carry Environment

The carry debate seems to be critical in explaining the valuation compression, so let us look deeper into what is already happening versus what is potentially being feared. The monetization environment is not weak currently. We have already charted the sharp spike in net realizations, especially in Q4.

On the other hand, Net Accrued Performance Revenues (NAPR), which is an indicator of carry earned but not realized, has moved up from ~$6.3b to ~$6.7b through 2025. So, the questions are not around value creation, but rather the potentially uneven timing for realizations. Looking back, uneven timing is a given in the business, with the lumpier realization seen in Q4. Management commentary in the Q4 earnings call indicates a reopening of capital markets and what was being seen as early stages of normalization. But we are still in a regime where consistent deal activity is not happening. Since realizations remain subject to the macro conditions, a worsening macro scenario (higher for longer rates in a stagflation scenario, risk-off sentiment, and uneven capital markets) becomes a cause for concern ahead, even if the 2025 story looks about alright.

There is no question around value creation in my view, as carry is still being created and accumulated. The Q4 spike in realizations does show that whenever windows open, exits and realizations can accelerate sharply, but the uncertainty around timing and lumpiness is real. That shifts the debate from value to visibility (delaying earnings versus slowing them).

In fact, the reason I remain on the Buy side despite the visibility issue is that value creation is still intact. Plus, the very macro situation that raises slower exit cycle fears also means a stronger deployment phase. Higher rates and tighter liquidity could mean opportunity for stronger deal terms and attractive entry valuations for new deployments, especially in credit and private markets. Blackstone has already deployed ~$138b in 2025 in what could be higher-return vintages. According to commentary, we can already see selective deployment opportunities showing up -

We also took advantage of volatility in markets to sign or close 8 privatizations during the year in private equity and real estate, including in the fourth quarter, medical technology company, Hologic, for $18 billion.

Valuations And Opportunity

Over the past one year, the stock is down by ~24%. Depending on the cutoff periods (because the latest FRE numbers are as of December 31, 2025), we will still see the share prices are mostly down at a time when FRE has gone up by ~9%. That points to the degree of valuation compression in FRE multiple terms over 2025 and the early months of 2026.

What has potentially changed is the way markets were pricing in stable fees and performance earnings earlier, expecting carry to normalize over time. Now, the view is potentially heavy discounting applied to the carry profile (so a better FRE commands a lower multiple overall). Therein lies an opportunity to accumulate more at current valuations. What markets are penalizing is an uncertain exit environment ahead and monetization visibility rather than magnitude.

While the monetization efficiency was still seen in Q4 2025, it was in selective windows. With a worsening macro being factored in now, carry realizations could be pushed out even further, keeping reported earnings uneven. However, delayed earnings does not equate to lost value, especially for long-term investors willing to sit through near-term cycles. Accrued carry is still looking good, and there is no issue visible in investment performance across strategies.

I maintain a Buy rating for Blackstone (probably higher conviction for the valuation-induced better entry point now). Investors will need to monitor realization momentum and NAPR trends to gauge whether the accrued earnings versus delayed realization thesis remains intact. Deployment arbitrage in current conditions and FRE growth should also continue to be important. If realizations slow down meaningfully while NAPR also stops growing, that would indicate a genuine slowdown in value creation or monetization. I do not expect that situation to arise given how the Q4 numbers show strong realizations whenever windows appear. As this continues to be supported and becomes more even, rerating, alongside FRE increments, should push Blackstone significantly higher from current levels.

data.title: HYT: Improved Valuation, But Bonds Likely To Remain Under Pressure
data.description:
The BlackRock Corporate High Yield Fund (HYT) is a closed-end fund that aims to provide its investors with a high level of current income. The fund does reasonably well at this task, as it boasts an 11.02% current yield at today’s share price, which it obtains through investments made in a portfolio of junk bonds. While this yield seems likely to attract many investors, the fact that the fund earns its income from bonds could be problematic. Over the past several weeks, bond yields have been rising, which has weighed upon the share prices of just about any bond fund. Furthermore, there could be reasons to doubt that bonds in general will be able to deliver positive real returns going forward, let alone reasonable returns. While junk bonds will almost certainly be better than U.S. Treasuries in this respect, they also have much higher default risk, and it is very possible that the real returns that they deliver will not adequately compensate investors for the risks that they are assuming. With that said, it might still be possible for an actively managed bond fund to deliver real returns through trading bonds, as my concerns are mostly centered around a buy-and-hold strategy in which an investor purchases a bond when it is first issued and holds it through maturity. There are some actively managed bond funds that employ such a buy-and-hold strategy, though, so we should pay close attention to the composition and strategy of any fund before purchasing it. Let us take a look at the BlackRock Corporate High Yield Fund and see if it could be a reasonable holding today or if the income that it provides is not worth the risk.

About the BlackRock Corporate High Yield Fund

The fund’s website describes the fund thusly:

The Trust seeks to achieve its objectives by investing, under normal market conditions, at least 80% of its assets in domestic and foreign high yield securities, including high yield bonds, corporate loans, convertible debt securities, and preferred securities which are below investment grade quality.

This description is much more expansive than just junk bonds. After all, it explicitly states that the fund’s portfolio might include things such as corporate loans and convertible debt. These securities are completely different animals than junk bonds. For example, corporate loans are almost always floating-rate securities. Unlike bonds, the price of a corporate loan does not really change much in response to macroeconomic events or interest rate movements. As we can see here, the iShares Floating Rate Bond ETF, which tracks a portfolio of investment-grade corporate loans, has been almost flat for nearly all of the past ten years:

With these securities, it is the coupon payment that changes with interest rate movements and not the price of the security. Furthermore, the amount of money that the coupon pays out usually changes based on short-term interest rates that are easier for central banks to control, rather than long-term interest rates that are typically market-based. As such, it is not generally a good idea to hold corporate loans whenever the Federal Reserve (or whatever central bank manages the currency that the bond is denominated in) is in the process of reducing interest rates. This is because the amount of money that the bond pays its investors will be declining, and it will not deliver price appreciation that might offset the reduction in income.

Convertible bonds also exhibit very different market performance than junk bonds. As the name suggests, these bonds can be converted into common equity securities of the issuing company under certain circumstances. Due to this feature, these bonds will frequently rise in price whenever the common stock of the issuing company rises. This is due to the fact that the conversion feature becomes more valuable as the price of the company’s stock rises. This makes sense because the convertible bond can be converted into common stock at a fixed ratio regardless of the common stock price. In some ways, it functions much like a call option with certain bond traits (such as coupon payments). As many readers likely know, call option prices also rise whenever the common stock that the call option is written against rises. This results in convertible bonds performing very differently from most bonds in the market. For example, this chart shows the performance of the iShares Convertible Bond ETF (ICVT), which tracks the price of an index of convertible bonds, compared to the S&P 500 Index (SP500) and both investment-grade bonds (AGG) and junk bonds (JNK) over the past ten years:

As we can clearly see, the convertible bond index delivered a performance that was a bit worse than common stocks but far better than either of the two bond indices. This is ordinarily what we would expect from such securities. We should also note that these are the only types of bonds that I would recommend for an investor who wants to buy the bond and hold it until maturity, due to the fact that their common equity-linked upside should do a better job of delivering positive real returns over the long term than will ordinary bonds.

Finally, the website claims that the BlackRock Corporate High Yield Fund will occasionally invest in preferred stocks. This is also very surprising for a junk bond fund due to the fact that preferred securities are technically considered a form of equity and not debt. Admittedly, preferred securities do function similarly to bonds in some respects due to the fact that preferred securities almost always pay a dividend that is either fixed or variable, depending on some interest rate benchmark. Preferred stock dividends are not tied to the performance of the issuing company as common stock dividends are. That is similar to the way a bond or other debt security works. However, if a company fails to pay a preferred stock dividend, then it is not considered to be a default on a debt obligation. Furthermore, most preferred stocks do not have maturity dates, and the only way for investors to get their money back is by selling the preferred security. This is more similar to the way common equities function, not bonds.

As such, it is somewhat surprising that the BlackRock Corporate High Yield Fund claims to be able to invest in these alternative security types, as including them in the portfolio will change the performance of the fund, perhaps drastically. However, the fund’s annual report states that its exposure to these alternative types of securities was somewhat limited as of December 31, 2025. A junk bond is anything rated BB or lower. As we can clearly see in the table, this level is when the default rates start to increase dramatically. A jump in the ten-year cumulative default rate from 3.80% to 12.90% between BBB (the lowest investment-grade rating) and BB (the highest speculative-grade rating) is a pretty large change that clearly shows how much higher the risk of a junk bond defaulting is when compared to investment-grade bonds.

As we can see here, nearly all of the portfolio of the BlackRock Corporate High Yield Fund is invested in junk bonds:

As this chart shows, the BlackRock Corporate High Yield Fund only has 5.54% of its total assets invested in investment-grade bonds. All of the rest of the bond portfolio is invested in junk bonds. While the fund does have 3.45% of its assets invested in unrated securities, these are usually issued by entities that would have speculative-grade ratings if the issuer would have gotten the bond issue rated. After all, there is a significant interest savings with investment-grade bonds, so if the issuer was capable of getting such a rating, then it probably would have gone through the time and expense to have its securities rated.

What this all means is that many of the securities that are held by the BlackRock Corporate High Yield Fund have a pretty high chance of defaulting at some point in their lives. This is especially true for the CCC-rated and D-rated securities that comprise 14.83% of the fund’s portfolio. As these securities have historically had a greater than 50% ten-year cumulative default rate, the fact that the BlackRock Corporate High Yield Fund has such a high exposure to them may be somewhat concerning to more risk-averse investors. Fortunately, there are two ways through which the fund attempts to limit its losses in the event of a default.

  1. The fund’s portfolio contains 1,211 positions. This limits the proportion of the fund’s total assets that is invested in any individual position, which limits its losses in the event of a default. After all, if a bond defaults, the fund’s losses will generally be limited only to the money that it had invested in that single position.
  2. As an actively managed fund, HYT can sell bonds if the issuing company is deteriorating. This is not an index fund that simply buys and holds bonds. We can see that the fund does frequently trade bonds in the fact that it had a 54% turnover rate during the twelve-month period that ended on December 31, 2025. While selling off bonds of a financially deteriorating company may not prevent the fund from taking any losses at all, it might be able to limit the losses that it takes by selling the bonds before the default actually occurs.

With respect to the second point, the BlackRock Corporate High Yield Fund has historically had a turnover ratio that is high enough to suggest frequent buying and selling of bonds:
